title: QUIC Interop Retry Child-Process Contract Architecture
purpose: Describe the smallest managed child-process boundary that proves one valid Retry can be emitted by the server, observed and replayed by the client, and completed on the real managed path without widening public support or broad token behavior.
design_summary: The child-process retry path is split across one small server prelude and one existing client replay seam. The server side owns a one-shot Retry emission inside `QuicListenerHost`, using the library-owned Retry integrity helper to send a valid Retry with its own source CID and opaque token on the first Initial. The client side already owns Retry receipt parsing and one replay through `QuicClientConnectionHost` and `QuicConnectionRuntime`. After the replayed Initial arrives, the listener threads the original destination connection ID and Retry source connection ID into the local transport parameters so the existing binding validation can complete on the managed path. The harness runner dispatches `retry` on both roles and exits `0` only after the post-Retry handshake completes. This does not widen public API, transfer, or broader TLS policy.
alternatives_considered: Treat retry as a client-only replay proof. Rejected because the child-process harness must prove both issuance and completion on the real server path.
Expose a new public retry knob on `QuicListenerOptions`. Rejected because it would widen the public boundary.
Fake retry by skipping the unsupported fallback. Rejected because it would not prove the managed replay handoff.
edge_cases_and_constraints: Exactly one valid Retry is in scope.
No multi-Retry behavior or general token persistence is in scope.
The replayed Initial must use the Retry source connection ID and token emitted by the server prelude.
The slice stays child-process only and does not widen the public API, `IsSupported`, transfer, or broader TLS policy.
data_and_state_considerations: The listener host holds one retry bootstrap state record only until the replayed Initial is admitted. The client host continues to retain original destination connection ID and Retry token only across one replay. The Retry token is opaque state, not generalized NEW_TOKEN persistence.

supplemental_sections.heading: Scope
supplemental_sections.content: This slice covers the managed child-process testcase that proves the server can emit one valid Retry, the client can observe and replay it once, and the real managed handshake can complete on the replayed path. It does not claim transfer enablement, byte delivery, EOF, or broader token behavior.

supplemental_sections.heading: Boundary Split
supplemental_sections.content:
Harness-owned code stays limited to `InteropHarnessRunner.cs`, process lifecycle, testcase dispatch, and child-process proof.
Library-owned code keeps `QuicConnection`, `QuicConnectionRuntime`, `QuicClientConnectionHost`, `QuicListenerHost`, and the replay/binding mechanics.

risks: A later change could accidentally widen the test into generic token lifecycle support instead of one-shot Retry replay.
A synthetic bypass would hide the real child-process sequencing gap rather than proving the managed seam.
The runtime already owns the replay and binding primitives, so the remaining risk is in harness sequencing and proof discipline, not in inventing new protocol behavior.
